In my opinion, biportal technique is a suitable approach, especially for anatomical cementectomies.
This biportal approach has several benefits.
First, we can put a grasper into a pleural cavity through the camera port, improving the exposure and traction of the pertinent pulmonary segment.
Additionally, this manoeuvre decreases the number of instruments through the utility port, providing more comfortability to the sergeant.
And second, and even more important, we can switch both the camera and the stapler in two different ports.
It allows us to view the hillier structures from different perspectives.
Furthermore, we can insert the stapler from different angles, decreasing the tension over the sub-cementary vessels, which are especially fragile.

We decided to perform anterior sementectomy in order to avoid the pulmonary lobectomy and save as much parenchyma as possible, which is very, very important in the treatment of the pulmonary metastasis.
When we try to perform an anatomical sementectomy, the most important aspect is to know preparatively the anatomy of the hilary structures and his branches.
This anatomical knowledge will prevent vascular accidents and cutting round structures.
So let's explain in depth the different steps we should do for performing a right anterior sementectomy.
The first step when we perform a right anterior sementectomy is to dissect and to expose the right pulmonary vein.
For that, we start cutting the pleural flexion between the visceral pleura and the asthenal pleura.
At this point, we should take care of the front nerve in order to avoid any injuries.
Once we clearly identify the entire pulmonary vein, we should start dissection in the lower limit between the middle and the upper pulmonary vein.
This manoeuvre is going to be very practical for the next step.
We can use either the harmonic or the hook for doing this dissection in a safe way.
Once we finish with the lower side of the right upper pulmonary vein, we start the dissection of the upper side between the vein and the artery.
It is mandatory to expose the helium of the upper lobe from the ventral to the dorsal side before starting the dissection of the vascular branches.
In this way, we can control the main vessels more easily if there is a vascular accident at the level of the branches.
The right upper pulmonary vein presents a complex anatomy that we must know in depth.
If we cut a wrong structure, we can compromise the venous drainage of the pulmonary segment.
Variations of the venous pattern are numerous.
There are two more common alternatives for the venous drainage of the anterior segment S3.
The first option, like in this case, is the presence of a large V3 that is the lowermost branch of the central vein.
The second option is one or two small ascending veins called V3a and V3b branching from the central vein that are easily recognized as they come directly from the anterior segment.
The upper and the middle lobes are retracted backward to expose the upper vein in a usual manner.
Then, the V3 is clearly seen, dissected and encircled with elastic tape.
The second step is probably one of the key points during an S3 cementectomy.
The division of the minor fissure between the right upper and middle lobes is crucial.
When the fissure is completely fused, as in this case, we start dividing the fissure partially with a stapler.
The inferior blade is placed between the right upper and the middle lobe, and the superior blade should follow the plane between S3 and the middle lobe.
Then we have to dissect the pulmonary artery in the fissure.
Usually there are several interlobar lymph nodes which we should remove in order to expose clearly the pulmonary artery at this level.
Once we remove the lymph nodes, we have to clean all the layers until the adventitia.
We should identify the artery for the middle lobe and sometimes we can also see some small arterial vessels going to the upper lobe.
Once the PA is completely dissected, we are going to cross the minor fissure.
For this, we have to pull the lung forward back and to insert the dissector carefully just above the middle lobe vein.
The previous lymph nodes removal is going to make this manoeuvre much easier.
So, the dissector is inserted from the anterior to the posterior side.
At this point, basically, we must be careful, especially with the middle lobe artery.
So, we should avoid abrupt movements in order to prevent vascular accidents.
Additionally, if we find the right pathway, we will prevent to insert the dissector through the parenchyma.
This is one of the most frequent mistakes during this step.
Once we pass the dissector through the minus fissure, we have to grasp the parenchyma towards the anterior side, until we can see the tip of the dissector.
Sometimes, we have to repeat this manoeuvre several times until we reach the other side.
If we find the right way, we will prevent possible postoperative ehrlichs.
After encircling the minor fissure with elastic tape, we will insert an endogea with a 60 bar per load in order to divide the parenchyma between the upper and the middle lobe.
A careful dissection will prevent possible vascular injuries, especially at the level of the middle lobe artery.
A vascular accident at this level could be difficult to repair.

When the artery is small and the space is limited, the artery can be sealed using an ultrasonic device, metallic or plastic clips, or just directly tying a knot.
The main risk of these different systems is the possibility of slipping during manipulation of the lung.
That's why vascular staplers are preferable whenever possible.
